questions.question_text: A purchase agreement is accepted on Thursday evening. By when must the broker deposit the earnest money?
questions.correct_answer: First legal banking day after acceptance
distractors: Within 3 business days of acceptance
Within 24 hours of the signed contract
By the end of the following business week
questions.explanation: Under SDCL 36-21A-80, earnest money must be deposited on the first legal banking day after acceptance of the contract, or as otherwise agreed to in writing by the parties. For a Thursday acceptance, this would typically be Friday.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits $200 of personal funds into the trust account to cover monthly bank service fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, personal funds to cover bank charges are allowed
distractors: No, this constitutes commingling of funds
Yes, up to $500 of personal funds is always allowed
No, bank fees must be paid from a separate account
questions.explanation: The sole exception to SD's commingling prohibition allows a broker to deposit personal funds to cover bank-assessed charges or fees. These broker funds must be tracked separately on a ledger labeled 'broker equity' or 'broker funds.'

questions.question_text: What type of reconciliation does South Dakota require for trust accounts?
distractors: Annual audit by a CPA
Quarterly bank statement review
Semi-annual balance confirmation
questions.explanation: SDCL 36-21A-80 requires three-way monthly reconciliation: the bank statement, trust ledger, and check register must all be reconciled at least monthly. Failure to reconcile is a violation of the law and common grounds for discipline.

questions.question_text: Which three records must be reconciled in the monthly trust account review?
questions.correct_answer: Bank statement, trust ledger, and check register
distractors: Bank statement, tax records, and closing files
Trust ledger, MLS records, and agency agreements
Check register, listing agreements, and tax returns
questions.explanation: The three-way reconciliation compares the bank statement, trust ledger, and check register. All three must match monthly. This is one of the most commonly tested trust account topics and a frequent area of SDREC violations.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits $500 of personal funds in the trust account to maintain a minimum balance with the bank. No bank fees are involved. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, personal funds are only for bank charges
distractors: Yes, up to $1,000 of personal funds is fine
Yes, if documented on a separate ledger line
No, but it only warrants a written warning
questions.explanation: The commingling exception is narrow — personal funds may ONLY be deposited to cover bank-assessed charges or fees. Maintaining a minimum balance for the broker's convenience is not an approved exception and constitutes commingling.

questions.question_text: A real estate transaction falls through and the buyer and seller disagree about who gets the earnest money. How can the broker disburse the funds?
questions.correct_answer: Written agreement of all parties or court order
distractors: The broker decides based on the contract terms
Funds go to the seller automatically after 30 days
The SDREC makes the final disbursement decision
questions.explanation: When a deal doesn't close, disputed trust funds can only be disbursed through written instructions from all parties or by court order. The broker may NOT disburse disputed funds unilaterally — even if the broker believes one party is clearly entitled.

questions.question_text: How long must a South Dakota broker retain transaction records after the final closing?
distractors: 3 years
5 years
7 years
questions.explanation: South Dakota requires brokers to retain all transaction records for a minimum of 4 years from the final closing. This includes agency agreements, contracts, seller's disclosure, purchase agreements, addenda, closing statements, and all relevant forms.

questions.question_text: Trust funds remain in the broker's trust account until the transaction is:
distractors: Approved by the lender
Past the inspection period
Recorded at the county office
questions.explanation: Trust funds stay in the account until the transaction is either consummated (closed) or terminated (deal falls through). If terminated with a dispute, funds require mutual written agreement or court order for disbursement.

questions.question_text: A buyer believes they are entitled to the earnest money after a seller breaches the contract. The seller disagrees. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Hold funds until mutual agreement or court order
distractors: Release funds to the buyer since seller breached
Split the funds equally between both parties
Send the funds to the SDREC for resolution
questions.explanation: Even when a broker believes one party is entitled, they cannot unilaterally disburse disputed funds. The broker must hold the money in trust until all parties provide written authorization or a court orders release. The SDREC does not resolve earnest money disputes unless the broker acted recklessly.

questions.question_text: Which documents are included in the 4-year record retention requirement for SD brokers?
questions.correct_answer: Agency agreements, contracts, and closing statements
distractors: Only the final purchase agreement and the deed
Only financial records and all bank statements
Only documents signed by both parties involved
questions.explanation: The 4-year retention covers a broad range: agency agreements, contracts, seller's disclosure, purchase agreements, addenda, closing statements, and all relevant transaction forms. Brokers should retain every document related to the transaction.
